prompt:
Passage 1
A regional electrical supply warehouse recently changed how it handles low-stock alerts. Previously, employees checked shelf quantities at the end of each week and reported items that appeared to be running low. This process worked for slower-moving products, but fast-moving items could sell out before the next weekly check.
The warehouse now uses an inventory system that sends automatic alerts when certain products fall below a set quantity. Purchasing staff can review the alerts each morning and decide whether to reorder items. The system also allows managers to adjust alert levels for products that sell more quickly during certain seasons.
Since the change, the warehouse has reduced the number of times commonly requested items are unavailable. Managers believe the system helps them respond sooner to changes in demand.

What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: The warehouse stopped selling fast-moving products.
choices.B: Automatic low-stock alerts helped the warehouse manage inventory more effectively.
choices.C: Employees now check shelf quantities only once per year.
choices.D: Purchasing staff no longer review inventory needs.

explanation: The passage explains how automatic low-stock alerts help the warehouse reorder sooner and reduce unavailable items.

prompt:
Passage 2
NOTICE
Beginning July 1, employees working in the fabrication area must place scrap metal in the labeled collection bins located near each workstation. Scrap should be separated by type whenever possible.
The updated procedure is intended to improve recycling, reduce clutter, and make it easier for employees to keep walkways clear. Under the previous process, scrap was sometimes left on carts or placed in general trash containers, making removal less efficient.
Supervisors will review the procedure with each shift during the first week of July.

What is the purpose of the notice?

choices.A: To announce a new scrap metal collection procedure
choices.B: To close the fabrication area
choices.C: To explain changes to employee pay
choices.D: To introduce a new product catalog

explanation: The notice announces the new procedure for collecting scrap metal in labeled bins.

prompt:
Passage 3
Many companies use purchase approvals to control spending and make sure orders are necessary. A purchase approval process may require employees to describe what they need, explain why it is needed, and receive permission from a supervisor before placing an order.
This process can prevent duplicate purchases and help companies stay within budget. For example, one department may already have extra supplies that another department plans to order. A supervisor reviewing the request may identify the overlap before money is spent.
However, approval systems must be designed carefully. If the process is too slow, employees may not receive needed materials on time. A useful system balances cost control with the practical needs of daily work.

What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Purchase approvals can help control spending but must not slow work too much.
choices.B: Employees should order supplies without explaining why.
choices.C: Companies should approve every purchase automatically.
choices.D: Budgets are unrelated to ordering materials.

explanation: The passage explains that purchase approvals help control spending, but the system must remain practical enough not to delay needed materials.

prompt:
Passage 4
Before refrigerated display cases became common in stores, many perishable foods had to be kept in back-room storage areas or sold quickly before spoiling. Customers often had less opportunity to view fresh products before making a purchase.
Refrigerated display cases changed retail food sales by keeping items cool while also making them visible to customers. Stores could present dairy products, meats, prepared foods, and other perishables in organized displays. This helped customers compare items and allowed stores to keep products available for longer periods.
Modern refrigerated cases include improved temperature controls and lighting. Store employees still need to monitor temperatures and clean cases regularly to maintain food quality and safety.

What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Refrigerated display cases changed how stores present and preserve perishable foods.
choices.B: Stores no longer sell perishable foods.
choices.C: Customers prefer products they cannot see.
choices.D: Temperature monitoring is unnecessary in food sales.

explanation: The passage focuses on how refrigerated display cases helped stores keep perishable food cool and visible to customers.

prompt:
Passage 5
A city bus garage recently introduced a driver feedback system for reporting route conditions. Drivers can submit notes about construction, damaged bus stops, traffic delays, or areas where passengers frequently ask for schedule information.
Before the system was introduced, drivers often mentioned these issues informally to supervisors at the end of a shift. Some concerns were addressed quickly, but others were forgotten or repeated by several drivers before action was taken.
The new system allows supervisors to review reports by route and issue type. Transit managers say the information helps them identify recurring problems and coordinate with maintenance crews, planning staff, or city departments when needed.

What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Bus drivers no longer communicate with supervisors.
choices.B: A driver feedback system helps the bus garage track and respond to route issues.
choices.C: Transit managers stopped reviewing route conditions.
choices.D: Passenger schedule questions are no longer important.

explanation: The passage explains how a driver feedback system helps track route issues and coordinate responses.

prompt:
Passage 6
Many workplaces use clear labeling to help employees identify materials, tools, storage areas, and safety information. Labels may include product names, part numbers, hazard warnings, expiration dates, or handling instructions.
Clear labels reduce the chance of mistakes. If two containers look similar but hold different materials, a readable label helps employees choose the correct one. Labels can also save time by making supplies easier to locate.
However, labels must be maintained. A label that is torn, faded, covered, or outdated may create confusion instead of preventing it. For this reason, some organizations include label checks as part of routine inspections.

What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Clear labels support safety and efficiency but must be maintained.
choices.B: Labels are useful only when they are unreadable.
choices.C: Workplaces should avoid labeling storage areas.
choices.D: Employees should rely only on container appearance.

explanation: The passage explains that clear labels help safety and efficiency, but only if they are maintained.

prompt:
Passage 7
Many communities operate household hazardous waste collection events. These events allow residents to bring items such as old paint, solvents, pesticides, batteries, and certain cleaning products to a designated location for proper handling.
The goal is to keep hazardous materials out of regular trash, storm drains, and household storage areas where they may create risks. Trained staff at collection events sort items and send them to appropriate disposal or recycling facilities.
Public education is important because residents may not always know which items require special handling. Clear instructions about accepted materials, drop-off times, and packaging requirements can make the events safer and more efficient.

What is the passage mainly about?

choices.A: Household hazardous waste events help residents dispose of certain materials safely.
choices.B: Residents should pour hazardous liquids into storm drains.
choices.C: Cleaning products can never create risks.
choices.D: Collection events are used only for regular household trash.

explanation: The passage explains how hazardous waste collection events help residents dispose of risky household materials safely.

prompt:
Passage 8
An adult training program recently revised its attendance policy for a pre-apprenticeship course. Instructors had noticed that students who missed several early classes often struggled with later lessons, even when they tried to catch up on their own.
The revised policy requires students to contact the program office after any absence. Staff then provide information about missed assignments, available review sessions, and upcoming deadlines. The goal is not to punish students for missing class, but to help them reconnect with the course before they fall too far behind.
Program leaders believe the new policy may be especially helpful for adult learners who balance school with work, family responsibilities, and transportation challenges.

Why was the attendance policy revised?

choices.A: Students who missed early classes often struggled with later lessons.
choices.B: Instructors wanted to cancel the pre-apprenticeship course.
choices.D: The program stopped offering review sessions.

explanation: The policy was revised because students who missed several early classes often struggled with later lessons.
